summary: Jeff Atwood highlights Adam Bosworth's influential article about lessons learned from the web, emphasizing simplicity, loose coupling, and crowd wisdom as key architectural principles. Beyond the technical architecture, Atwood argues that the web browser's user interface innovations were equally critical to the web's success. He praises the single-click interface, the back button, and the forced simplicity of one-task-per-page design. The post also touches on Bosworth's argument for RSS as a replacement for XML's complexity, while noting the potential conflict of interest given Bosworth's employment at Google during the Google Base era.
key_points: The web succeeded because of 'unintuitive lessons': simple text formats beat complex binary ones, staleness is acceptable, and crowd wisdom works
Browser UI innovations (single-click, back button, single-task pages) were as important as architectural principles in popularizing the web
The back button is described as 'the most profound innovation in user interface in the last ten years'
RSS 2.0 is proposed as a simpler, more web-friendly replacement for XML's complexity
Webby UI principles became formalized as 'Inductive User Interface' and influenced client applications and operating systems
money_quotes: The browser popularized the web by making it dead simple to use
The back button. Easily the most profound innovation in user interface in the last ten years. Don't like where you are? Go back to where you were. It's totally natural.
Simple, relaxed, sloppily extensible text formats and protocols often work better than complex and efficient binary ones
Since websites are slow to load and can't display much at once, designers were forced to stick to one task per page
The wisdom of crowds works amazingly well
It is acceptable to be stale much of the time
key_insight: The web's success came not just from architectural simplicity and loose coupling, but equally from browser UI innovations that made complexity invisible to users.
